planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; snowy woodrush is one of the most ornamental woodrushes for bright, clumping foliage and showy white flower clusters in early summer.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; snowy woodrush tolerates dry shade better than many shade plants once established, though it looks best with consistent moisture. Luzula nivea is a clumping, evergreen woodrush native to central and southern European mountain forests, producing bright green, grass-like leaves edged with fine white hairs that give it a frosted, silvery appearance, and showy, loose clusters of brilliant white flowers in early summer that are among the showiest of any rush or sedge and are excellent as cut flowers. The fine-textured evergreen foliage provides year-round interest in shaded borders, and the plant is useful as an underplanting for shade trees, along stream banks, and as a textural contrast to broad-leaved shade perennials like hostas and astilbe.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; a western North American native closely related to Solomon's seal, with attractive spreading rhizomes and cheerful spring flowers followed by ornamental berries in fall.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; Hooker's fairy bell grows slowly but spreads naturally by rhizomes to form loose colonies under trees. Disporum hookeri is a western North American native woodland perennial that resembles a smaller Solomon's seal, with arching, unbranched to slightly branched stems 18–36 inches tall bearing oval, parallel-veined leaves and nodding, creamy-white, bell-shaped flowers in early spring followed by red to orange ornamental berries in fall that wildlife eat avidly. It is an elegant and under-used alternative to true Solomon's seal for Pacific Northwest and shaded mountain gardens; in the Southeast, it prefers cool, moist, shaded conditions and is best suited to upper zones of the native range. The berries are not for human consumption.

planting.note: Plant container plants or root cuttings in spring in dry to moist, well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; this native Florida Panhandle plant is exceptionally drought-tolerant and produces spectacular hummingbird-attracting scarlet flowers from late summer through fall.

directions: Plant in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants are highly drought-tolerant and require little supplemental irrigation in zone 8–10. Scarlet calamint is a native Florida and Georgia Panhandle herb in the mint family (Lamiaceae) with an extremely limited natural range in the coastal plain, but it is increasingly available in native plant nurseries and is among the most hummingbird-attractive natives for the Deep South; it produces arching stems with small, slightly fuzzy, aromatic leaves and clusters of brilliant scarlet-red tubular flowers from late July through October that are irresistible to ruby-throated hummingbirds and are rarely touched by deer. The plant has a bushy, somewhat sprawling habit and is excellent in native pollinator gardens, dry herbaceous borders, and naturalistic plantings in zones 7–10.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall or very early spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; bellwort is a spring ephemeral woodland perennial that goes dormant by midsummer — pair with ferns and hostas to fill the void.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in early fall or very early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade, setting rhizomes 2 inches deep; large-flowered bellwort is slowly stoloniferous and forms loose colonies over time but is never invasive. Uvularia grandiflora is one of the most beautiful and underutilized native spring wildflowers of eastern North American deciduous forests; it emerges in early spring with perfoliate (stem-piercing) leaves and nodding, lemon-yellow, lily-like flowers with twisted petals 1–1.5 inches long that dangle from arching stems in April and May, making it one of the most charming and elegant of all woodland wildflowers. The plant goes dormant by midsummer; pair with late-emerging hostas or ferns to cover the gap. It naturalizes beautifully under deciduous trees and is an excellent companion for Virginia bluebells and wild ginger.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist woodland soil for natural cold stratification and spring germination; this native shade perennial naturalizes readily from self-seeding and is among the brightest and longest-blooming yellow wildflowers for shaded sites.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; cold stratification occurs naturally over winter and seeds germinate in early spring. Wood poppy is a native eastern North American woodland perennial in the poppy family with deeply lobed, blue-green, poppy-like leaves and large (1.5–2 inch), brilliant golden-yellow flowers with four petals that bloom from early spring through summer in shaded conditions, making it one of the brightest and most prolific yellow-flowering shade plants for the eastern US — a standout with spring bloomers like wild ginger, bleeding heart, and ferns. It self-seeds freely without becoming invasive, gradually naturalizing into a cheerful drift under deciduous trees. All parts of the plant exude orange sap when broken; do not ingest.
